-JPZ21IAAAAJ.keywords: robotics, visual servoing, robot modeling and control, motion planning, sampling-based motion planning, multi-robot coordination, active perception, visual tracking, biomimetic aerial robotics, heterogeneous multi-robot task allocation
-JPZ21IAAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
Seth Hutchinson is a senior researcher and professor of robotics at **Northeastern University**, leading a research group focused on advancing the theory and practice of robot perception, planning, and control. His lab bridges rigorous algorithmic development with real-world robotic systems, emphasizing **visual servoing**, **motion planning**, and multi-robot coordination. Hutchinson's work is distinguished by its blend of foundational textbooks and influential tutorials alongside applied projects (from sensing and tracking to biomimetic flight), and it consistently emphasizes <u>robust perception-to-action</u> pipelines and <u>real-time control</u> for autonomous systems. His publications and textbooks serve as canonical references in the field, and his research agenda places particular value on *principled, implementable methods* that connect theory to practice.

---

## Research Areas
Hutchinson's research spans several core areas of robotics, with sustained contributions to both theory and systems. He is best known for foundational work in **visual servo control** (e.g., "A tutorial on visual servo control" (2002), "Visual servo control. I. Basic approaches" (2006), and "Visual servo control. II. Advanced approaches" (2007)), which develops control laws that close the loop on image measurements for manipulation and mobile platforms. Complementing perception-driven control, he has authored influential texts on kinematics and dynamics ("Robot Modeling and Control" (2006); "Robot modeling and control, 2nd edition" (2020)) and coauthored broad treatments of motion planning ("Principles of Robot Motion" (2005)). His work also addresses multi-robot coordination and planning under uncertainty ("Optimal motion planning for multiple robots having independent goals" (2002); "Path planning for permutation-invariant multirobot formations" (2006)), sensing and exploration strategies ("Planning exploration strategies for simultaneous localization and mapping" (2006); "Planning Sensing Strategies in a Robot Work Cell with Multi-Sensor Capabilities" (1989)), and practical performance issues such as real-time tracking and estimator design ("Real-time vision, tracking and control" (2000); "Estimating uncertainty in SSD-based feature tracking" (2002)). More recent and applied contributions include biomimetic flight platforms ("A biomimetic robotic platform to study flight specializations of bats" (2017); "Bat Bot (B2), a biologically inspired flying machine" (2016)) and long-duration autonomy and task-allocation for heterogeneous teams ("Robot ecology: Constraint-based control design for long duration autonomy" (2018); "A resilient and energy-aware task allocation framework for heterogeneous multirobot systems" (2021)).

## Notable Works
- "Robot Modeling and Control" (2006) — a widely used textbook that synthesizes kinematics, dynamics, and control for manipulators and mobile robots; it has become a standard reference for both teaching and research in robot modeling and control, and its later edition (2020) continues to influence curriculum and practice.  
- "A tutorial on visual servo control" (2002) — a seminal tutorial paper that established a coherent conceptual framework for image-based and position-based visual servoing, clarifying prerequisites from robotics and computer vision and guiding two decades of follow-on research.  
- "Principles of Robot Motion: Theory, Algorithms, and Implementations" (2005) — a comprehensive treatment of motion planning that connects rigorous theory to algorithmic implementation, widely cited for its role in making planning algorithms accessible and practical for robotic systems.

## Academic Background
Seth Hutchinson has a multi-decade academic career with research and teaching roles spanning leading robotics institutions. Over the years he has collaborated with and published alongside researchers at the University of Illinois and Georgia Tech, and he is currently affiliated with **Northeastern University**, where he continues to supervise students and lead research projects. Hutchinson is the author and coauthor of foundational textbooks and highly cited tutorials that have shaped robotics education and research; his work has accrued over 33,000 citations, reflecting broad influence across control, perception, planning, and multirobot systems. His publication record (from early work on sensing strategies and planning in the late 1980s and 1990s to recent contributions on robotic grasping and long-duration autonomy) demonstrates a sustained impact on both theoretical foundations and real-world robotic applications.

1GsJvtwAAAAJ.keywords: Cyber Forensics, Systems Security, Malware Analysis, Memory Forensics, Android Forensics, Software Supply Chain Security, Package Manager Attacks, Log-based Attack Reconstruction, Symbolic Root Cause Analysis, Web Application/CMS Abuse
1GsJvtwAAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
Brendan D. Saltaformaggio is an **Associate Professor** in the School of Electrical and Computer Engineering at the **Georgia Institute of Technology**, where he leads a research group within the ECE department focused on systems- and evidence-driven security research. His work centers on **cyber forensics**, **systems security**, and **malware analysis**, with an emphasis on building tools and methodologies that help defenders and investigators reason about complex, real-world attacks. In particular, his group advances techniques for *memory forensics*, *log-based attack reconstruction*, and *supply-chain and software ecosystem security*, addressing challenges that span from low-level binary instrumentation to large-scale empirical measurement. Key technical themes in his work include <u>attack provenance and reconstruction</u>, <u>automated root-cause analysis</u>, and <u>abuse of web and package ecosystems</u>.

---

## Research Areas
Saltaformaggio’s research spans the intersection of systems research and applied security. He has developed methods for reconstructing multi-stage attacks from dispersed telemetry (e.g., the graph-based reconstruction techniques in "Hercule"), and advanced the state of the art in memory forensics and binary analysis (work on GUI and photographic evidence recovery from memory images, RetroScope-style screen recovery, and platform-agnostic binary rewriters like RevARM). He studies the privacy and information-leakage risks of mobile systems, including traffic-analysis attacks that infer fine-grained app activities over encrypted channels, and device-wide user-activity sequencing from Android memory images. His recent and highly cited work investigates ecosystem-level threats: empirical measurement and characterization of _supply-chain attacks against package managers_, large-scale studies of malicious plugins and web-app abuse used by malware, and automated remediation techniques for vulnerable open-source components embedded in application binaries. Across these threads, he emphasizes practical systems that scale to real-world workloads and support forensic investigation and incident response.

## Notable Works
- "Towards Measuring Supply Chain Attacks on Package Managers for Interpreted Languages" (2021, 255 citations) — A large-scale empirical study that quantified how package managers are abused by adversaries, demonstrating attack patterns and proposing measurement methodologies to detect and mitigate supply-chain compromises in language ecosystems.  
- "Hercule: Attack story reconstruction via community discovery on correlated log graph" (2016, 248 citations) — Introduced a correlated-log-graph approach to stitch dispersed log footprints into coherent multi-stage attack narratives, enabling automated attack-story reconstruction for intrusion analysis.  
- "Eavesdropping on Fine-Grained User Activities Within Smartphone Apps Over Encrypted Network Traffic" (2016, 148 citations) — Demonstrated that passive observers can infer fine-grained in-app user actions from encrypted network traffic, exposing privacy risks in modern mobile apps and motivating countermeasures for sensitive application flows.

## Academic Background
Brendan Saltaformaggio completed advanced graduate training in computing and has established a research program at Georgia Tech that bridges systems engineering and digital forensics. His publications appear in top security and systems venues and collectively have accrued over **1,855** citations, reflecting broad impact across academia and practice. His work has been supported through collaborations with industry partners and by competitive research funding, and he regularly mentors graduate students and postdoctoral researchers who pursue forensic, malware, and systems-security topics. He is an active member of the security research community, contributing tools, datasets, and empirical studies that have informed defenses against supply-chain compromise, mobile- and cloud-based abuse, and stealthy multi-stage attacks.

-ZqZyP0AAAAJ.keywords: Human-Computer Interaction, Health, HCI for Development, Digital Mental Health, Maternal Health, Menstrual Health Education, Mobile Communication, Intersectional Design, AI in Global Health, Community Health Workers
-ZqZyP0AAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
Neha Kumar is an Associate Professor at **Georgia Tech**, where she leads an interdisciplinary research group focused on the intersection of **Human–Computer Interaction (HCI)** and **health**. Her lab conducts field-driven, design-oriented research that emphasizes participatory and context-sensitive methods; she is particularly known for combining *qualitative ethnography*, *mixed-methods studies*, and design interventions to address health, wellbeing, and social justice in under-resourced settings. Across her work she foregrounds <u>community-led approaches</u>, <u>assets-based design</u>, and <u>decolonial perspectives</u> to rethink how computing can support equitable forms of care and public health.

---

## Research Areas
Kumar’s research spans several tightly related areas within HCI and health. She investigates mobile and social computing practices in the Global South (e.g., smartphone and WeChat use, emoji/sticker communication), the design and evaluation of technology for maternal and menstrual health (e.g., community-led video education, Menstrupedia), and the situated deployment of digital tools for community health workers and frontline health systems. She also critically examines emerging technologies — including AI and large language models — for digital mental health and public health datafication, emphasizing the potential benefits and harms of these systems. Methodologically, her papers draw on *ethnography*, *participatory design*, and *design justice* lenses to operationalize concepts such as <u>intersectionality</u>, <u>care</u>, and <u>citational justice</u> in technology design and evaluation.

---

## Notable Works
- "Goodbye Text, Hello Emoji: Mobile Communication on WeChat in China" (2017) — A qualitative study that documents the rapid shift from text to pictorial communication on WeChat and analyzes how emoji and stickers reshape interpersonal expression across rural, small-town, and urban contexts.
- "The ins and outs of HCI for development" (2016) — An empirical survey and analysis of the HCI4D literature that synthesizes trends, methodological practices, and gaps in work focused on under-served and under-resourced populations, providing a roadmap for responsible, context-aware design.
- "Mobile phones for maternal health in rural India" (2015) / "Projecting health: community-led video education for maternal health" (2015) — A body of mixed-methods work and an implemented intervention (Community-led Video Education) that explores how mobile technologies and locally produced video can disseminate maternal and newborn health information in rural Uttar Pradesh.

---

## Academic Background
Neha Kumar’s publication record demonstrates sustained engagement with HCI venues and interdisciplinary collaborations addressing technology, health, and social inequities. Her work is highly cited (total citations: **6,494**) and appears in leading conferences and journals in HCI and CSCW, reflecting both empirical field studies and conceptual contributions (e.g., on post-growth HCI, decolonial approaches to digital mental health, and design for intersectional wellbeing). Based at Georgia Tech, she maintains an active lab and web presence (http://www.nehakumar.org/) and has built a body of work characterized by deep field engagement in India, comparative studies across Global South contexts, and critical analyses of emerging technologies in health. Her scholarship has influenced design practices for community health, menstrual health education, safety technologies, and the ethical deployment of AI in public health, positioning her as a prominent voice in socially engaged HCI.

06hgT8gAAAAJ.keywords: Security, Operating System, Systems, Trusted Execution Environments (SGX), Fuzzing, Concolic Execution, Side-Channel Attacks, Control-Flow Integrity, Persistent Memory & File Systems, Memory Safety
06hgT8gAAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
Taesoo Kim is a Professor of Computer Science at the **Georgia Institute of Technology**, where he leads a research group that focuses on systems-oriented security research. His lab develops practical defenses, analysis tools, and instrumentation for real-world software and hardware platforms. His primary research spans the intersection of **systems** and **security**, with particular emphasis on <u>trusted execution</u>, <u>program analysis and fuzzing</u>, and <u>microarchitectural and OS-level defenses</u>. He is best known for building *practical, deployable techniques* that address both performance and security trade-offs across operating systems, persistent memory, and cloud environments.

---

## Research Areas
Taesoo Kim’s work lies at the nexus of operating systems, program analysis, and security. He has advanced automated vulnerability discovery and testing through hybrid techniques combining fuzzing and symbolic/concolic execution (e.g., QSYM), and he has designed new OS primitives and harnesses to improve fuzzing of complex targets (e.g., harness synthesis, file-system fuzzing approaches). A large strand of his research investigates the security and limitations of Intel SGX and other trusted execution environments, documenting side channels (branch shadowing, Rowhammer-based denial-of-service), proposing mitigations (T-SGX, SGX-shield), and enabling practical deployment of TEEs for network and cloud services. He has also contributed foundational systems-security work on cache side-channel defenses in multi-tenant cloud settings (STEALTHMEM), kernel and control-flow protections (KASLR-breaking attacks, CFI refinements, PAL for pointer authentication), and persistent-memory-aware storage and indexing (SplitFS, Recipe). Across these topics he emphasizes scalable, engineerable solutions—building tools and abstractions (libmpk, APISan, fuzzing frameworks) that are both evaluated on real workloads and broadly useful to the research and practitioner communities.

## Notable Works
- QSYM: "QSYM: A practical concolic execution engine tailored for hybrid fuzzing" (2018, 758 citations) — introduced a scalable concolic execution engine integrated with fuzzing to overcome coverage and path-explosion limitations of each technique when used alone, substantially improving bug-finding effectiveness on real-world binaries.  
- SGX side-channel analyses and defenses: "Inferring fine-grained control flow inside SGX enclaves with branch shadowing" (2017, 724 citations) and "T-SGX: Eradicating Controlled-Channel Attacks Against Enclave Programs." (2017, 552 citations) — exposed practical information-leakage and control-flow inference techniques against Intel SGX and proposed mitigations to harden enclave execution against controlled-channel and branch-shadowing attacks.  
- Cloud and microarchitectural defenses: "STEALTHMEM: System-Level protection against Cache-Based side channel attacks in the cloud" (2012, 522 citations) — provided a system-level approach to mitigate cache-based side channels in multi-tenant cloud environments, influencing subsequent work on isolation and microarchitectural defenses.

## Academic Background
Taesoo Kim is a senior faculty member in the School of Computer Science at the **Georgia Institute of Technology**, where he directs a research group working on systems security. He has an extensive publication record across top-tier systems and security venues and his work has been widely cited (Total citations: **12,368**), reflecting broad impact on both applied security and systems research. Over his career he has developed widely used tools and artifacts (e.g., QSYM, libmpk, SplitFS) and collaborated across OS, architecture, and security communities to translate research into deployable primitives. His homepage (https://taesoo.kim/) hosts his publications, software releases, and group information. He has supervised numerous graduate students and postdocs, and his research consistently targets problems of practical relevance to cloud providers, OS developers, and the security community.

1kW15d8AAAAJ.keywords: software engineering,science policy,articificial intelligence,software reuse,requirements engineering,software design,software maintenance,software engineering education,systems engineering,bioinformatics

1vwuDM4AAAAJ.keywords: Cryptography, security, secure computation, zero knowledge proofs, garbled circuits, secure multi-party computation, oblivious transfer extension, oblivious PRF, private set intersection, privacy-preserving databases
1vwuDM4AAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
Vladimir Kolesnikov is a faculty researcher in the College of Computing at the **Georgia Institute of Technology**, where he leads a research group focused on *applied cryptography* and *privacy-preserving systems* (see his homepage: https://www.cc.gatech.edu/~vlad/). His work centers on foundational and practical techniques for <u>secure computation</u>, with particular emphasis on **garbled circuits**, **secure multi-party computation (MPC)**, and **zero-knowledge proofs**. He combines theoretical constructions with systems-oriented evaluations to produce protocols and implementations that are both *asymptotically principled* and *practically deployable*.

---

## Research Areas
Kolesnikov’s research spans the design, optimization, and application of cryptographic protocols for private computation and data protection. He is well known for contributions to the theory and practice of garbled-circuit based two-party and multi-party computation (e.g., the *Free XOR* optimization and subsequent garbling improvements), oblivious primitives such as *oblivious PRFs* and *OT extension* optimizations, and protocols for *private set intersection/union* that trade off public-key cost for symmetric-key efficiency. His work also addresses bringing private computation into realistic domains: scalable private database systems (e.g., *Blind Seer*), privacy-preserving classification and medical applications, and secure pattern matching. More recently he has contributed to non-interactive zero-knowledge constructions via the *MPC-in-the-head* paradigm and to techniques that amortize or structurally change garbling (e.g., fleXOR, stacked garbling, and amortized garbled circuits) to reduce communication and computation overheads for large statements and repeated executions.

---

## Notable Works
- "Improved garbled circuit: Free XOR gates and applications" (2008) — introduced the *Free-XOR* optimization that dramatically reduced the cost of evaluating XOR gates in Yao-style garbled circuits, enabling substantial practical speedups in two-party secure computation and becoming a de facto standard optimization in subsequent GC implementations.  
- "A pragmatic introduction to secure multi-party computation" (2018) — a widely-cited survey and systems-oriented treatment that bridges theory and practice in MPC, explaining key techniques and trade-offs and helping to spur practical adoption and system-building efforts.  
- "Efficient batched oblivious PRF with applications to private set intersection" (2016) — developed lightweight, batched OPRF protocols and demonstrated how symmetric-key based techniques yield practical, scalable private set intersection/union protocols, influencing a generation of PSI/PSU constructions.

---

## Academic Background
Vladimir Kolesnikov has a long and prolific record of contributions to cryptography and systems security dating back to the early 2000s, including both academic publications and patented inventions (several patents and collaborative projects with industry partners such as Bell Labs / Alcatel‑Lucent are listed among his works). His research has been published at top venues across theory and systems (Crypto, Eurocrypt, IEEE S&P / Oakland, CCS, and others), and his papers have accumulated a substantial impact (total citations: **8,780**). He is an active member of the cryptography community—regularly collaborating on cross-cutting problems in secure computation, advising students and postdocs, and participating in program committees and community efforts to translate cryptographic research into deployable systems.

_Z12v2IAAAAJ.keywords: Database Systems, Database Design, Schema Integration, Conceptual Data Modeling, Entity–Relationship Modeling, Vertical Partitioning, Distributed Databases, Temporal Databases, Data Mining, Association Rule Mining
_Z12v2IAAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
Shamkant B. Navathe is a **Professor of Computer Science** at the *Georgia Institute of Technology* and a senior member of the university's **Database Systems research community**. He leads and contributes to research and educational efforts focused on the design, modeling, and implementation of database systems. His work spans both foundational textbook authorship and technical research on practical database design methods; key emphases include <u>database design and modeling</u>, <u>schema integration</u>, <u>data fragmentation and distribution</u>, and <u>data mining for association discovery</u>.

---

## Research Areas
Navathe's research portfolio covers a broad spectrum of database systems topics. He is best known for contributions to **conceptual modeling and database design**, as embodied in work such as *Conceptual database design: an entity-relationship approach* and the multiple editions of *Fundamentals of Database Systems*. He has developed formal and practical methods for <u>schema integration</u> (e.g., *A comparative analysis of methodologies for database schema integration*, *Methodology for view integration in logical database design*), and for *vertical partitioning* and *fragmentation* to support performance and distributed design (e.g., *Vertical partitioning algorithms for database design*, *Vertical partitioning for database design: a graphical algorithm*). Navathe has also advanced research in temporal databases (*A temporal relational model and a query language*), automated support for physical design (e.g., *Adaptive and automated index selection in RDBMS*), and data mining techniques for association discovery (*An Efficient Algorithm for Mining Association Rules in Large Databases*, *Mining for strong negative associations in a large database of customer transactions*). His work additionally shows interdisciplinary application of database technology to biological databases (MITOMAP) and health-care analytics (hospital readmission and social risk factors), illustrating a blend of theoretical foundations with applied systems and tools.

## Notable Works
- *Fundamentals of Database Systems* — the widely used textbook coauthored by Navathe that synthesizes core theory and practical design techniques for modeling, languages, and DBMS implementation; a foundational teaching and reference work adopted internationally.
- *An Efficient Algorithm for Mining Association Rules in Large Databases* (1995) — introduced a scalable, practical algorithmic approach for discovering association rules in large transaction databases, helping to catalyze work in transactional data mining and market-basket analysis.
- *A Comparative Analysis of Methodologies for Database Schema Integration* (1986) — provided systematic study and methodologies for integrating heterogeneous schemas, laying groundwork for later automated and semi-automated schema integration techniques used in federated and enterprise-level database design.

## Academic Background
Navathe has a long and influential academic career as a faculty member in computer science at the *Georgia Institute of Technology*, during which he has authored highly cited textbooks and a substantial corpus of research papers that together have earned him more than **34,600** citations. His publications trace back to the late 1970s and show sustained leadership in database design, schema integration, fragmentation and distribution, temporal models, and data mining. His textbooks have been translated into multiple languages and released in many editions, underlining a major impact on database education worldwide. Throughout his career he has combined theoretical advances with practical tools and interdisciplinary collaborations (notably in bioinformatics and healthcare), supervised and mentored students, and contributed to the database research community through methodology, software tools, and widely used instructional materials.

_ZIAy3cAAAAJ.keywords: human-computer interaction, information visualization, visual analytics, sensemaking, semantic interaction, interactive machine learning, analytic provenance, large high-resolution displays, cognitive bias mitigation, interactive model steering
_ZIAy3cAAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
Alex Endert is an **Associate Professor** at **Georgia Tech**, where he leads research in the university’s Visual Analytics community (see his lab homepage: http://va.gatech.edu/endert). His work sits at the intersection of *human–computer interaction*, *information visualization*, and *visual analytics*. He studies how people reason with data and how interactive visual systems can couple human cognition with computational models to support *sensemaking* and decision making. Central to his agenda are the design and evaluation of interfaces that enable <u>semantic interaction</u>, <u>interactive steering of analytic models</u>, and the use of large, high-resolution displays to externalize and scaffold analytic thought. His scholarship is widely cited (total citations ~**7,731**) and spans empirical studies, interaction techniques, and system-building.

---

## Research Areas
Endert’s research addresses core problems in how people interact with, steer, and understand analytic systems. He investigates the role of physical and digital space in cognition (e.g., the influence of large, high-resolution displays on collaborative and individual sensemaking in “Space to think” and related display work), develops methods for coupling user actions to model parameters through <u>semantic interaction</u> (for example, “Semantic interaction for visual text analytics” and “Semantic interaction for sensemaking”), and studies the integration of machine learning into interactive visual analytics workflows (“The state of the art in integrating machine learning into visual analytics”, “Mixed-initiative visual analytics using task-driven recommendations”). He also works on tracking and representing analytic provenance (“Characterizing provenance in visualization and data analysis”), detecting and mitigating cognitive biases in interactive analysis (“Warning, bias may occur”, “Toward a design space for mitigating cognitive bias in vis”), and augmenting visualization with automated explanatory artifacts (e.g., NLG-based *interactive data facts*). Methodologically, his publications combine system design, user studies, and frameworks for evaluation and user experience, producing both new interaction paradigms (e.g., Visualization by Demonstration, V2PI) and empirical evidence about their effectiveness.

---

## Notable Works
- "Space to think: large high-resolution displays for sensemaking" (2010) — a foundational empirical and conceptual study showing how expanded visual workspaces support external cognition and spatial organization during analytic tasks (507 citations).  
- "Semantic interaction for visual text analytics" (2012) — introduces and formalizes the idea of letting users manipulate visual representations as a mechanism to implicitly steer underlying statistical models, enabling more natural coupling of cognition and computation (327 citations).  
- "The state of the art in integrating machine learning into visual analytics" (2017) — a comprehensive survey and synthesis that maps techniques, challenges, and research directions for combining interactive visualization with machine learning models in visual analytics systems (341 citations).

---

## Academic Background
Alex Endert is an Associate Professor at Georgia Tech and is affiliated with the university’s Visual Analytics research efforts (his lab page: http://va.gatech.edu/endert). Over his career he has published prolifically in top venues across *HCI*, *information visualization*, and *visual analytics*, accruing over **7,700** citations that reflect broad impact across empirical, systems, and framework contributions. His work has been supported through collaborations and competitive research funding (academic and industry partnerships), and he routinely leads interdisciplinary projects that bridge visualization, machine learning, and cognitive science. He has served as advisor and mentor to graduate students and has contributed to community-facing syntheses (surveys and design spaces) that have influenced subsequent work in areas such as provenance, bias mitigation, and human-centered machine learning.

_cAwG4gAAAAJ.keywords: Cybersecurity, AI, Blockchain, DSP, Computers, Internet of Things, Cloud computing, Smart contracts, Privacy, VLSI
_cAwG4gAAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
Vijay Madisetti is a **Professor of Cybersecurity & Privacy** at the Georgia Institute of Technology, where he leads a multidisciplinary research group focused on the intersection of **cybersecurity**, **distributed ledger technologies**, and systems-level engineering for connected devices. His lab pursues *applied research and system design* that bridges theory and practice across embedded systems, cloud infrastructures, and secure data-driven applications. Key themes in his work include <u>secure and scalable blockchain architectures for industrial IoT</u>, <u>privacy-preserving machine learning and AI</u>, and foundational methods in <u>digital signal processing (DSP)</u> and real-time embedded systems. His professional homepage: http://www.ece.gatech.edu/~vkm

---

## Research Areas
Madisetti’s research spans several tightly connected areas. In distributed ledger technologies and blockchain he has investigated architectures and methods for deploying blockchains in industrial settings (see "Blockchain platform for industrial internet of things", 2016) and devised approaches to tune blockchain scalability, decentralization, and security for fast, low-cost transaction processing (multiple patent-family works, 2018–2019). In the Internet of Things and cloud systems he has authored practical guides and frameworks ("Internet of Things - A Hands-On Approach", 2014; "Cloud Computing - A Hands-On Approach", 2014) and developed cloud-based analytics and maintenance platforms for sensor-rich industrial systems ("Analyzing massive machine maintenance data in a computing cloud", 2011). His long-standing contributions to signal processing and real-time implementation—theoretical and systems-level—are captured in major edited volumes and textbooks (Digital Signal Processing Handbooks, 1997 & 2010) and in work on VLSI and embedded DSP implementations ("VLSI digital signal processors", 1995). Across these topics he emphasizes systems integration, security and privacy by design, and practical methods for deploying scalable, interoperable solutions in industry and healthcare (e.g., "A cloud-based approach for interoperable electronic health records (EHRs)", 2013).

---

## Notable Works
- "Blockchain platform for industrial internet of things" (2016) — a highly cited study that articulates architectures and deployment models for integrating **blockchain** with industrial IoT applications (manufacturing automation, prognostics, supply-chain), highlighting challenges of interoperability, latency, and trust.
- "Digital Signal Processing Handbook" (2010) — an authoritative edited reference consolidating theoretical foundations and practical algorithms for DSP, with emphasis on implementation issues that connect to embedded and VLSI realizations.
- "Internet of Things - A Hands-On Approach" (2014) — a practical, educational treatment of IoT technologies that has influenced both research and teaching by providing hands-on approaches to sensing platforms, connectivity, cloud integration, and application design.

---

## Academic Background
Professor Madisetti is an IEEE Fellow with a long, interdisciplinary career reflected in more than 8,500 citations and influential publications spanning DSP, VLSI, cloud computing, IoT, blockchain, and cybersecurity. At Georgia Tech he holds a faculty appointment in engineering and has authored/edited multiple textbooks and handbooks, led industry-oriented research projects, and is an inventor on a number of blockchain- and security-related patents (2018–2023). His publication record (books, high-impact conference and journal papers, and patents) and sustained cross-domain work indicate active collaborations with industry and domain stakeholders in manufacturing, healthcare, and cloud services, as well as mentoring graduate students and postdocs in both foundational and applied topics.

_pdhnmwAAAAJ.keywords: Randomized Algorithms, Discrete Probability, Machine Learning, Glauber dynamics, Spectral independence, Markov chain mixing time, Entropy factorization, Spin systems (Gibbs distributions), Hamiltonian Monte Carlo, Sampling and approximate counting
